# Results

This section presents the outcomes of applying {{< acr DL >}} methods to detect {{< acr SM >}} using {{< acr UAV >}}-based imagery and point cloud data. The analysis explored two key approaches: semantic segmentation of {{< acr RGB >}} and {{< acr MS >}} imagery, and instance segmentation of {{< acr LiDAR >}} point clouds (@fig-wf_overall). The primary question was whether automated methods can reliably identify individual {{< acr SM >}} canopy in dense native forests. This is a task with direct conservation implications, as accurate mapping of rare and threatened species such as {{< acr SM >}} is essential for targeted management efforts against {{< acr MR >}}.

Detection of {{< acr SM >}} was moderately successful using semantic segmentation. Under certain conditions, models demonstrated promising detection ({{< acr F1 >}} scores of 0.65–0.81 on favourable sites), though performance declined significantly with smaller training datasets (best {{< acr F1 >}}=0.56) or when training across multiple sites simultaneously (best {{< acr F1 >}}=0.51). While {{< acr LiDAR >}}-based instance segmentation methods showed promise, both methods produced results that were not sufficient for inclusion within the workflows of this study. Below, we present detailed results from the semantic segmentation analysis, including investigation of hyperparameters, followed by a qualitative assessment of why the {{< acr LiDAR >}} approaches require further development.

## U-Net Semantic Segmentation

### Single-site

When trained on data from a single site, U-Net was able to detect {{< acr SM >}} canopies with moderate to good performance. However, performance varied considerably between sites, with {{< acr F1 >}} scores ranging from 0.46 to 0.81. Amount of available training data significantly improved performance (Pearson p=0.017 and R^2^=0.996; @fig-linear_regression). A variety of model configurations and different band combinations were tested as input to identify the best performing configuration. The following sections present this optimisation work, followed by a section on the best models and how they performed on unseen data.

:::: {#fig-linear_regression fig-scap="Linear regression of annotated training area vs. F1 score."}

::: {layout="[-0.15, 0.7, -0.15]"}
```{python}
import os
import sys
import geopandas as gpd
import pandas as pd

# Make local plotting helper available
sys.path.insert(0, '../2_1_figures/results')
from linear_regression_plot import plot_annotation_area_vs_f1

# Access reserve paths for annotation areas
sys.path.insert(0, '../4_1_modules')
from config.paths import use

reserves = ['ESK', 'KAU', 'BUS', 'HAM']
best_f1_scores = {
    'ESK': 0.55,  # A1
    'KAU': 0.41,  # A2
    'BUS': 0.75,  # A3
    'HAM': 0.27   # H1
}

rows = []
for reserve_code in reserves:
    r = use([reserve_code])[0]
    gpkg_path = os.path.join(r.GIS, 'swamp_maire_poly.gpkg')
    train_zone = os.path.join(r.GIS, 'bbox.gpkg')
    gdf = gpd.read_file(gpkg_path, layer='maire_poly_rgb')
    train_gdf = gpd.read_file(train_zone, layer='unet_training_zone')
    gdf = gdf.clip(train_gdf)
    if gdf.crs and gdf.crs.is_geographic:
        gdf = gdf.to_crs('EPSG:2193')
    annotation_area = gdf.geometry.area.sum()
    rows.append({
        'Site': reserve_code,
        'Annotation_Area_m2': annotation_area,
        'Best_F1_Score': best_f1_scores[reserve_code]
    })

df = pd.DataFrame(rows)
plot_annotation_area_vs_f1(df)
```

:::

Linear regression of annotated {{< acr SM >}} area (m^2^) within the training zone vs. F1 score trained on RGB imagery (@tbl-bands) for each site. Each point represents a site, and the regression line is shown in red with R^2^=0.966.
::::

#### Hyperparameter Configuration

To identify which configuration had the greatest impact on detection accuracy, three key hyperparameters were systematically tested: the loss function (which guides how the model learns), the {{< acr LR >}} (which controls the magnitude of model weight adjustments during training), and class weights (which penalise misclassification of the underrepresented {{< acr SM >}} class more heavily). This systematic comparison of hyperparameters (@tbl-hyperparams) revealed differences in model performance. 

{{< acr DICE >}} achieved the highest mean {{< acr F1 >}} scores (0.56±0.14 for {{< acr RGB >}} imagery, 0.54±0.25 for MS$_{rel}$), outperforming {{< acr WBCE-D >}} (0.50±0.20 / 0.45±0.26) and {{< acr WBCE >}} (0.48±0.27 / 0.46±0.17). {{< acr LR case=sentence >}} had the most substantial impact on performance. Models trained with an initial {{< acr LR >}}=0.02 outperformed those with {{< acr LR >}}=5×10^-5^. When using {{< acr DICE >}} {{< acr F1 >}} scores dropped from 0.56±0.14 to 0.13±0.09 ({{< acr RGB >}}) and from 0.54±0.25 to 0.13±0.11 (MS$_{rel}$). The lower {{< acr LR >}} produced random predictions with extremely high recall (up to 0.98) but negligible precision (0.07–0.14). This means that virtually any pixel was classified as {{< acr SM >}} (@sfig-LR5e-5). Class weighting improved {{< acr WBCE-D >}} performance: {{< acr F1 >}} gained with each incremental step adjusting the weights from 1 to 10 to 50 in both {{< acr RGB >}} and MS$_{rel}$ (@tbl-weight). However, the difference from 10 to 50 is marginal when visually inspecting results (@sfig-predictions_single_weight_comparison). 


::: {#tbl-hyperparams layout=[[1],[-0.3],[1],[-0.3],[1]]}

```{python}
#| label: tbl-loss
#| tbl-cap: "Results from tested loss functions ({{< acr WBCE >}}, {{< acr DICE >}} (no weight) and {{< acr WBCE-D >}}) with weight=10 and {{< acr LR >}}=0.02."

import pandas as pd
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '../2_1_figures/results')

csv_path='../2_1_figures/results/summary.csv'
df = pd.read_csv(csv_path)

from metrics import table_loss_function_comparison
table_loss_function_comparison(df)
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-weight
#| tbl-cap: "Results from assigning weights 1, 10 and 50 for {{< acr SM >}} class using {{< acr WBCE-D >}}."

from metrics import table_weight_comparison
table_weight_comparison(df)
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-lr
#| tbl-cap: "Results from initial {{< acr LR plural=true >}} (0.02 and 5x10^-5^) in combination with a {{< acr LR >}}-scheduler with {{< acr WBCE-D >}} using weight=10."

from metrics import table_learning_rate_comparison
table_learning_rate_comparison(df)
```

Comparison of hyperparameters on {{< acr SM >}} detection performance (mean±std), tested on each site separately. Tested hyperparameters included loss function (**a**), class weight (**b**), and {{< acr LR >}} (**c**). 
:::

#### Band Combinations

Using base hyperparameters ({{< acr LR >}}=0.02, weight=10, {{< acr WBCE-D >}}), {{< acr RGB >}} imagery achieved the highest mean {{< acr F1 >}} score (0.50) across reserves, though, only marginally better than MS+IND$_{rel}$ (mean {{< acr F1 >}}=0.48). However, this was not consistent across all sites. At sites A1 and H1, {{< acr MS >}} band combinations outperformed {{< acr RGB >}} imagery: MS+IND$_{rel}$ achieved a notably high {{< acr F1 >}} of 0.64 on site A1. Performance varied substantially between the other sites: A3 showed the strongest and most consistent results across all band types ({{< acr F1 >}}=0.60–0.75), whilst site A2 consistently showed poor performance ({{< acr F1 >}}=0.08–0.41). Adding {{< acr RENDVI >}} to {{< acr MS >}} bands did not consistently improve performance, except for site A1 where it contributed to the best result of that site (@tbl-bands).

```{python}
#| label: tbl-bands
#| tbl-cap: "F1 scores for tested band combinations ({{< acr RGB >}}, MS$_{rel}$, IND$_{rel}$, MS+IND$_{rel}$) across reserves with {{< acr LR >}}=0.02, weight=10, and {{< acr WBCE-D >}}."

from metrics import table_band_comparison_pivot
table_band_comparison_pivot(df)
```


The best-performing model, trained on site A3 with MS$_{rel}$ bands and {{< acr DICE >}} (weight=1, {{< acr LR >}}=0.02), achieved an {{< acr F1 >}} of 0.81 and {{< acr IoU >}} of 0.68 (@tbl-best_single; @stbl-best_models_single_site_ESK, S4, S5, S6). Training metric progression is shown in @sfig-train_metrics_best. Site A3 consistently produced the strongest results, with six of the top ten models from this reserve. Performance was more variable on other sites: A1 reached a maximum {{< acr F1 >}} of 0.65, H1 achieved 0.59, and A2 showed the lowest maximum {{< acr F1 >}} of 0.46. 

```{python}
#| label: tbl-best_single
#| tbl-cap: "Top 10 individual single-site models ranked by F1 score. The top 10 models for each site individually can be found in Table S2."

from metrics import table_top_individual_models
table_top_individual_models(df, top_n=10)
```

Visual inspection of the predictions reveals clear differences in model behaviour (@fig-predictions_single). Site A3 predictions show high correspondence with ground truth, especially for {{< acr DICE >}}, which yielded cleaner edges, whereas {{< acr WBCE-D >}} models produced frayed edges and additional noise. Site A2 predictions were consistently smaller than ground-truthed area. Site H1 showed the poorest performance, with consistent misclassified patches and additional noise. {{< acr WBCE-D >}} with higher weights introduced more noise in both {{< acr RGB >}} and {{< acr MS >}} imagery predictions compared to {{< acr DICE >}}.


```{python}
#| label: fig-predictions_single
#| fig-cap: 'Predictions from the best-performing single-site models for the test zone for each reserve for a 15x15 m extent. Pink overlays indicate predictions on {{< acr MS >}} imagery, yellow overlays on {{< acr RGB >}} imagery. Model configurations are listed on the left; all were trained with a {{< acr LR >}} of 0.02. For example RGB-$L_{c}$-W10 reads as: "model trained on RGB bands using {{< acr WBCE-D >}} with a weight of 10".'
#| fig-scap: Predictions single-site.

import pandas as pd
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '../2_1_figures/results')

from predictions import plot_model_comparisons

models = [
    {'band_comb': 'ms_rel', 'lr': 0.02, 'loss': 'dice', 'weight': 1, 'label': r'MS$_{rel}$-$L_{Dice}$-W1'},
    {'band_comb': 'ms_rel', 'lr': 0.02, 'loss': 'bce_dice', 'weight': 10, 'label': r'MS$_{rel}$-$L_{c}$-W10'},
    {'band_comb': 'rgb', 'lr': 0.02, 'loss': 'dice', 'weight': 1, 'label': r'RGB-$L_{Dice}$-W1'},
    {'band_comb': 'rgb', 'lr': 0.02, 'loss': 'bce_dice', 'weight': 10, 'label': r'RGB-$L_{c}$-W10'},
]

# Generate comparison for all reserves
plot_model_comparisons(['ESK', 'KAU', 'BUS', 'HAM'], models, offset_x=[-1, 0, 0, 2], offset_y=[2, 0, 0, 1], figsize=(15,20))

```


### Multi-site

#### Band and Loss Functions

{{< acr RGB >}} bands outperformed {{< acr MS >}} bands in the multi-site setting, with the three best models all using {{< acr RGB >}} imagery (@tbl-multi_site). This trend confirms the single-site results, however, the performance gap between {{< acr RGB >}} and {{< acr MS >}} imagery is much more pronounced with {{< acr F1 >}}=0.51 ({{< acr RGB >}}) and {{< acr F1 >}}=0.35 ({{< acr MS >}}).

```{python}
#| label: tbl-multi_site
#| tbl-cap: "The performance metrics from the 10 best performing model configurations trained on the multi-site dataset, sorted by F1 score. A table with all models is available in @stbl-multi_site_all."

from metrics import table_multi_site_all
table_multi_site_all(df, max_rows=10)
```

Absolute-calibrated band combinations (MS$_{abs}$, IND$_{abs}$, MS+IND$_{abs}$) showed poor generalisation in multi-site training, with {{< acr F1 >}} scores ≤0.22 and subsequently also poor visual results (@fig-predictions-multisite). {{< acr DICE >}} on {{< acr MS >}} imagery showed low noise but exhibited substantial under-segmentation and misclassification, where both sites A1 ({{< acr F1 >}}=0.00) and A3 ({{< acr F1 >}}=0.07) were subject to these issues. This was also visible in training metrics progression, where {{< acr IoU >}} plateaued after ~30 epochs (@sfig-train_metrics_multi_site).

#### Best-performing Multi-site Models

Despite systematic optimisation, multi-site models achieved substantially lower performance than single-site models. The best multi-site configuration ({{< acr RGB >}} imagery, {{< acr WBCE-D >}}, weight=50) achieved an {{< acr F1 >}} of 0.51 and {{< acr IoU >}} of 0.34, representing a 37% reduction in {{< acr F1 >}} compared to the best single-site model. Only the {{< acr RGB >}} imagery model with {{< acr WBCE-D >}} achieved performance comparable to some single-site models ({{< acr F1 >}}=0.54). Across all configurations, multi-site predictions were notably noisier than their single-site counterparts (@fig-predictions-multisite), indicating that the model struggled to learn consistent {{< acr SM >}} characteristics across different forest structures and lighting conditions. Notably, visually one of the best results was on a site (A2) that showed poor results in the single-site setting, identifying {{< acr SM >}} accurately (RGB, $L_{c}$, weight=50; {{< acr F1 >}}=0.54; @fig-predictions-multisite).

```{python}
#| label: fig-predictions-multisite
#| fig-cap: 'Predictions from the best-performing multi-site models for the test zone for each reserve for a 15x15 m extent. Pink overlays indicate predictions on {{< acr MS >}} imagery, yellow overlays on {{< acr RGB >}} imagery. Model configurations are listed on the left; all models were trained with a {{< acr LR >}} of 0.02. For example RGB-$L_{c}$-W50 reads as: "model trained on RGB bands using {{< acr WBCE-D >}} with a weight of 50".'
#| fig-scap: "Predictions multi-site."
#| fig-pos: 'H'

import pandas as pd
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '../2_1_figures/results')

from predictions import plot_model_comparisons

models = [
    {'band_comb': 'ms_abs', 'lr': 0.02, 'loss': 'bce_dice', 'weight': 50, 'label': r'MS$_{abs}$-$L_{c}$-W50', 
     'multisite': ['KAU', 'ESK', 'BUS', 'HAM']},
    {'band_comb': 'ms_rel', 'lr': 0.02, 'loss': 'bce_dice', 'weight': 50, 'label': r'MS$_{rel}$-$L_{c}$-W50', 
     'multisite': ['KAU', 'ESK', 'BUS', 'HAM']},
    {'band_comb': 'ms_rel', 'lr': 0.02, 'loss': 'dice', 'weight': 1, 'label': r'MS$_{rel}$-$L_{Dice}$-W1', 
     'multisite': ['KAU', 'ESK', 'BUS', 'HAM']},
    {'band_comb': 'rgb', 'lr': 0.02, 'loss': 'bce_dice', 'weight': 50, 'label': r'RGB-$L_{c}$-W50', 
     'multisite': ['KAU', 'ESK', 'BUS', 'HAM']},
]

# Generate comparison for all reserves
plot_model_comparisons(['ESK', 'KAU', 'BUS', 'HAM'], models, offset_x=[-1, 0, 0, 2], offset_y=[2, 0, 0, 1], figsize=(15, 20))

```



## LiDAR Tree Segmentation

Two instance segmentation algorithms (TreeLearn and Treeiso) were tested to isolate individual tree point clouds from the forest point cloud. However, qualitative assessment of the two trialled segmentation methods revealed significant challenges with both approaches.

TreeLearn did not successfully segment individual trees and produced messy, near random looking segmentations (@fig-tree_instance_seg a-c). Treeiso performed better, producing recognisable tree segments, but segmentations frequently included portions of adjacent tree crowns, limiting their quality for species-specific classification (@fig-tree_instance_seg d-f). Given these limitations and the more promising results from {{< acr MS >}} imagery analysis, further development of {{< acr LiDAR >}}-based segmentation was not pursued within the timeframe of this thesis.

:::: {#fig-tree_instance_seg fig-scap='TreeLearn vs. Treeiso.'}

::: {layout="[1,-0.03,1,-0.03,1][1,-0.03,1,-0.03,1]"}

![Segmented points of segmentation 1 using TreeLearn.](../2_1_figures/tree_instance_seg/TreeLearn.png){#fig-a}

![Segmented points of segmentation 2 using TreeLearn.](../2_1_figures/tree_instance_seg/TreeLearn2.png){#fig-b}

![TreeLearn segmentation map in top-down nadir view.](../2_1_figures/tree_instance_seg/TreeLearnTop.png){#fig-c}

![Segmented points of segmentation 1 using Treeiso.](../2_1_figures/tree_instance_seg/TreeIso.png){#fig-d}

![Segmented points of segmentation 2 using Treeiso.](../2_1_figures/tree_instance_seg/TreeIso2.png){#fig-e}

![Treeiso segmentation map in top-down nadir view.](../2_1_figures/tree_instance_seg/TreeIsoTop.png){#fig-f}
:::

Comparison between extracted segmentation groups using TreeLearn (**a**--**c**) and Treeiso (**d**--**f**).
::::
